They passed swiftly by Tiness, too, a picturesque little hamlet, perched on a rocky eminence. Of all the passengers on board the MACQUARIE, the most to be pitied was Lord Glenarvan. He was rarely to be seen below. He could not stay in one place. His nervous organization, highly excited, could not submit to confinement between four narrow bulkheads. All day long, even all night, regardless of the torrents of rain and the dashing waves, he stayed on the poop, sometimes leaning on the rail, sometimes walking to and fro in feverish agitation. His eyes wandered ceaselessly over the blank horizon. He scanned it eagerly during every short interval of clear weather. It seemed as if he sought to question the voiceless waters; he longed to tear away the veil of fog and vapor that obscured his view. He could not be resigned, and his features expressed the bitterness of his grief. He was a man of energy, till now happy and powerful, and deprived in a moment of power and happiness. John Mangles bore him company, and endured with him the inclemency of the weather. On this day Glenarvan looked more anxiously than ever at each point where a break in the mist enabled him to do so. A few ice-floes drifted by us, occasionally peopled, as tourists throng a pleasure yacht, by penguins, and also by dusky seals, lying flat upon the white surfaces like enormous leeches. *Around the World in Eighty Days* is Jules Verne's most famous adventure, following the imperturbable English gentleman [[Phileas Fogg]] and his French valet [[Passepartout]] on a race around the globe. Fogg is introduced as "one of the most noticeable members of the [[reform-club|Reform Club]]" (AWED-001-002), a man of absolute routine.

It was on the island of Blejinie, the last of the Aleutian group at the extreme south of Behring Sea, that all the colonists of Fort Hope at last landed (FC-047-002).
first_appearance: MI-039

He turns castaway into commander, mock-hailed "Well, sir, here you are, Governor General of Algeria!" (OC-007-040), and when he sails off to explore he deputes his servant, who "was 'invested with governor's powers,' and took an affecting leave of his master" (OC-010-036).
- [[The Adventures of a Special Correspondent]] — the novel narrated wholly through his reporter's eye.
- [[Jaspar Hobson]] — the lieutenant amazed by her reappearance, for one wonders "what must Lieutenant Hobson have thought when he saw her leaning on Mrs Barnett" (FC-033-004).
He is a seasoned trapper who cannot be deceived, boasting "old trappers like us are not to be taken in" (FC-006-033).
- Louis Cornbutte, his son and the lost mate for whom the whole voyage is undertaken, wrings from him "an accent of the most profound despair" when feared dead (WAI-011-050).
